source.title: Pilot experiment retro and next phase planning
next_steps: Redwood: share per-model FP8/INT8 perf spreadsheet by 2026-12-20
Arcadia: run the 10k eval sweep on customer prompts and share results by 2026-12-23
Schedule canary rollout planning session w/ infra (30m) week of 2026-12-29
action_items: Ethan Park: upload end-to-end benchmark spreadsheet + runbook (due 2026-12-20)
Lina Gomez: triage top 5 regression examples and propose prompt fallback heuristics (due 2026-12-22)
Priya Singh: confirm VPC peering and KMS requirements for private pilot (due 2026-12-21)
Sanjay Rao: run 10k prompt quality sweep and surface SNR and hallucination counts (due 2026-12-23)
source.transcript:
Meeting Header:
Date: 2026-12-18
Start: 15:00 PST
Duration: 50 minutes
Attendees: Ethan Park, Lina Gomez, Arjun Patel - Redwood; Sanjay Rao, Priya Singh, Carlos Mendes, Marta Alvarez - Arcadia

Summary:
Quick retrospective on the quantization pilot sprint, review of FP8 vs INT8 perf/quality deltas across the safety-critical triage flows, and define next phase (canary + security checklist + runbook).

Topics:
- High-level perf deltas (latency/throughput/cost)
- Quality regressions and triage examples
- Fallbacks and routing policies (dedicated vs hosted)
- Infra/security gating for private pilot/VPC
- Next-phase plan: canary rollout + benchmarks + handoff

Action items (mentioned in-call): see metadata field for owners/dates.

Transcript:
[00:00] Ethan Park: ok everyone thanks for joining, um so this is our week-2 retrospective of the quant pilot, I want to focus on where we saw largest deltas and what the operational gating looks like for a canary.
[00:22] Sanjay Rao: yep thanks Ethan, from Arcadia side we've run the short-form triage suite — about 5k samples — and saw throughput improvements as expected, FP8 gave us roughly 1.9x token throughput on our A100-like fleet and int8 was about 2.1x, latency p50 down ~30% on FP8, ~36% on int8 with batching.
[00:44] Lina Gomez: mhm, yep those numbers track with the profiling we ran on Redwood Dedication — uh Redwood Dedicated — the runtime is favoring the int8 kernel selection for medium sequence lengths. Note: there was a CV for very long contexts where int8 kv-cache memory patterns got a little noisy.
[01:05] Marta Alvarez: when you say "noisy" can you clarify, is that memory instability or quality variance?
[01:10] Arjun Patel: so it's not a crash, more like the k-v cache alignment causes slightly higher variance in long-context attention weights which shows up as a small SNR drop, like 0.6-1.2 dB in our metrics for the 4 longest prompt buckets.
[01:30] Priya Singh: that matches an incident we saw on our infra run where one host peaked and then the request was routed to fallback. Not a big outage but it changed response ordering on some multi-turn flows, which is problematic for the clinician UI.
[01:47] Ethan Park: good to call out. On quality, Arcadia's 5k sweep showed FP8 avg metric delta -0.8% on our BLEU-like scoring and int8 -1.6%, is that right Sanjay?
[01:59] Sanjay Rao: yes, those are rough averages. But distribution matters — there are a handful (~30) of prompts where int8 introduced hallucinated entities that would be unacceptable in prod.
[02:14] Carlos Mendes: can you give an example? We need to see severity.
[02:18] Lina Gomez: sure, uh one example from the triage: prompt about patient medication summary — int8 sometimes omitted the allergy record in the summary or swapped ibuprofen for a different med string, it's subtle but safety-critical.
[02:33] Marta Alvarez: that's the kind of semantic drift we worry about. Is there a way to detect these at runtime?
[02:39] Arjun Patel: yes, two paths: 1) prompt-level quality checks with a lightweight verifier (embedding similarity, named-entity checks) and 2) routing policy where certain high-sensitivity routes go to FP8 or even fp16. We can set per-route SLOs and threshold-based fallbacks.
[02:58] Priya Singh: routing sounds good but also adds cost. What's the expected cost delta if we keep 20% of requests on fp16 as a safety net?
[03:07] Ethan Park: back-of-envelope, if int8 baseline cost is 1.0 unit, FP8 is about 1.25 and fp16 1.9; so keeping 20% on fp16 raises overall cost maybe ~18-20% depending on traffic mix. We can tune selector to only route high-risk types.
[03:27] Sanjay Rao: another point — quantization variance tends to show on longer token outputs; some of our summarization tasks produce 200–400 tokens, that tail is where int8 divergence is amplified.
[03:44] Lina Gomez: yes, we saw the same; we recommend a hybrid — int8 for short/structured responses, FP8 for long generation and any oncology/med-sum flows.
[03:55] Carlos Mendes: what about dedicated vs hosted? We were leaning private for compliance, but dedicated would be cheaper than full private.
[04:03] Arjun Patel: dedicated gives you reserved capacity and isolation without the full private control plane; private (VPC) is needed if you need customer-managed KMS and egress controls. Priya, you said you need HIPA-ish controls?
[04:15] Priya Singh: HIPAA compliance yes, audit logs, SSO integration. We also need KMS and no egress to public internet.
[04:23] Ethan Park: ok then Private is likely the right direction for the pilot if those constraints are firm. We can still do Dedicated-like reserved pools within the private deployment for cost predictability.
[04:34] Sanjay Rao: from a perf perspective, do we have runbook steps for when int8 shows a regression in production? Like rollback or partial route?
[04:43] Lina Gomez: yes, recommended runbook: detect via automated quality checks -> trigger A/B rollback to FP8 for that route -> alert SE/AE -> run small re-quant with adjusted calibration and rerun eval set. We'll draft the exact playbook.
[04:58] Marta Alvarez: about detection, do you have tooling that can run the verifier inline or is it async?
[05:03] Arjun Patel: both options. Inline verifier increases latency slightly but gives immediate protection; async verifier can flag a sample for re-eval and then we can run a safe re-route for subsequent requests. Depends on SLO.
[05:17] Carlos Mendes: latency impact numbers? If we add an inline named-entity check what's p95 delta?
[05:21] Lina Gomez: in our tests inline similarity check added ~12–25ms p95 depending on model size and batching. Usually acceptable for web UI flows but for sub-200ms use cases we'd do async.
[05:36] Ethan Park: cool. Ok let's talk rollout gating — checklist items: security docs (SOC2/ISO), KMS specs, network diagram, canary plan with traffic % and rollback triggers, regression sample list. Priya you mentioned you need an architecture whitepaper for security — we'll attach that.
[05:53] Priya Singh: yes please, also want a matrix of controls vs audit evidence.
[05:59] Ethan Park: noted. On canary traffic, propose 5% -> 20% over two weeks, with continuous quality monitoring. Lina and Arjun will own the monitoring hooks.
[06:11] Sanjay Rao: one ask — can you surface the per-sample logits or token-level confidence so we can build heuristics?
[06:16] Arjun Patel: yes we can expose token-level logprobs and top-k candidates; Redwood runtime can retain kv-cache metadata as well for debugging.
[06:25] Marta Alvarez: any surprising perf regressions aside from the long-context SNR?
[06:29] Lina Gomez: there was a micro-regression in batch size 1 p99 for small models under high concurrency — looks like a scheduling edgecase in kernel selection, we patched it in our internal branch and will push a production build early next week.
[06:44] Carlos Mendes: fantastic, timeline for that patch?
[06:47] Lina Gomez: aiming for 2026-12-21 rollout, no expected downtime.
[06:52] Ethan Park: perfect. Ok near end here, recap: Redwood to share detailed per-model spreadsheet (latency, throughput, cost, quality deltas) by 12/20; Arcadia to run 10k sweep by 12/23; Priya to confirm infra/KMS/VPC gating by 12/21; we schedule a canary planning follow-up wk of 12/29.
[07:12] Sanjay Rao: on the spreadsheet can you also include the sample-level regression examples so our clinicians can review?
[07:18] Ethan Park: yes we'll include top 50 flagged regressions and link to the triage doc.
[07:23] Marta Alvarez: thanks, also please include recommended prompt-level mitigations — few-shot anchors, explicit instruction to preserve allergies, etc.
[07:31] Arjun Patel: will do.
[07:33] Carlos Mendes: last q: any additional vendor lock risks if we move to private with Redwood?
[07:38] Ethan Park: minimal — models and weights are standard formats, and private deployment gives you data plane control; you can export checkpoints and run locally if you want. We'll document export and exit-paths in the contract appendix.
[07:51] Priya Singh: good.
[07:54] Ethan Park: excellent, thanks everyone. We'll follow up with items and schedule the canary planning.
[08:02] [call ends]

Meeting Header:

Duration: 50 minutes

[04:03] Arjun Patel: dedicated gives you reserved capacity and isolation without the full private control plane; private (VPC) is needed if you need customer-managed KMS and egress controls. Priya, you said you need HIPA-ish controls?
[04:15] Priya Singh: HIPAA compliance yes, audit logs, SSO integration. We also need KMS and no egress to public internet.

source.title: Arcadia Health - weekly check-in (streaming issue follow-up)
next_steps: Redwood to monitor us-east dedicated SSE stall metric for 7 days
Arcadia to add client read timeout + idempotency key for streaming retries
source.transcript:
Meeting Header
Date: 2026-02-03
Time: 2:00pm PT
Duration: 44 min
Attendees: Ava Chen, Alex Martinez (Redwood) | Nina Patel, Samir Desai (Arcadia Health)

Summary (auto)
- Discussed recent streaming freeze where responses stopped mid-generation.
- Reviewed gateway change (disable gzip for SSE, periodic flush).
- Agreed on client-side timeouts and retry behavior.

Transcript
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey Nina, Samir. Thanks for hopping on. Wanted to do a quick follow-up on the streaming freeze you saw last week.
[00:18] Nina Patel: Yeah, thanks. It was pretty bad. The stream would just, like, stop halfway through. No done event, and our UI spinner would spin forever.
[00:36] Alex Martinez: Right, that matches what we saw. On our side it looked like the connection stayed open but no bytes were being flushed.
[00:55] Samir Desai: Exactly. We werent getting an error, so our retry logic didnt trigger. We just sat there.
[01:14] Ava Chen: Since the change went out, have you seen any of those mid-generation freezes?
[01:22] Nina Patel: Since Monday, no. We ran a bunch of tests  like hundreds  and it hasnt frozen.
[01:40] Alex Martinez: Great. For context, the gateway was gzipping the event stream, which can buffer. We turned that off for text/event-stream and added a periodic heartbeat flush.
[02:05] Samir Desai: Makes sense. We also added a client read timeout so we dont hang forever if something like this happens again.
[02:22] Ava Chen: Good. Well keep monitoring and also document the recommended client-side behavior for streaming.
[03:01] Nina Patel: Cool. The main thing is we cant have the stream freeze mid-answer. That was killing agent productivity.
[43:10] Ava Chen: Recap: well watch metrics for a week and youll keep the timeout + idempotency retries. Well follow up next Tuesday.

[03:01] Nina Patel: Cool. The main thing is we cant have the stream freeze mid-answer. That was killing agent productivity.
[43:10] Ava Chen: Recap: well watch metrics for a week and youll keep the timeout + idempotency retries. Well follow up next Tuesday.
text:
Meeting Header
Date: 2026-02-03
Time: 2:00pm PT
Duration: 44 min
Attendees: Ava Chen, Alex Martinez (Redwood) | Nina Patel, Samir Desai (Arcadia Health)

Summary (auto)
- Discussed recent streaming freeze where responses stopped mid-generation.
- Reviewed gateway change (disable gzip for SSE, periodic flush).
- Agreed on client-side timeouts and retry behavior.

Transcript
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey Nina, Samir. Thanks for hopping on. Wanted to do a quick follow-up on the streaming freeze you saw last week.
[00:18] Nina Patel: Yeah, thanks. It was pretty bad. The stream would just, like, stop halfway through. No done event, and our UI spinner would spin forever.
[00:36] Alex Martinez: Right, that matches what we saw. On our side it looked like the connection stayed open but no bytes were being flushed.
[00:55] Samir Desai: Exactly. We werent getting an error, so our retry logic didnt trigger. We just sat there.
[01:14] Ava Chen: Since the change went out, have you seen any of those mid-generation freezes?
[01:22] Nina Patel: Since Monday, no. We ran a bunch of tests  like hundreds  and it hasnt frozen.
[01:40] Alex Martinez: Great. For context, the gateway was gzipping the event stream, which can buffer. We turned that off for text/event-stream and added a periodic heartbeat flush.
[02:05] Samir Desai: Makes sense. We also added a client read timeout so we dont hang forever if something like this happens again.
[02:22] Ava Chen: Good. Well keep monitoring and also document the recommended client-side behavior for streaming.
[03:01] Nina Patel: Cool. The main thing is we cant have the stream freeze mid-answer. That was killing agent productivity.
[43:10] Ava Chen: Recap: well watch metrics for a week and youll keep the timeout + idempotency retries. Well follow up next Tuesday.
